title: Rolling Conversation Summaries
concept: Periodically collapsing older turns of a long conversation into a running summary to bound context growth.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: what's the difference between extractive and abstractive compression?

options.text: Extractive pulls out key sentences verbatim; abstractive rewrites the content in new words
Extractive rewrites content; abstractive pulls out exact sentences
They are two names for the exact same technique

exercises.explain: Extractive compression selects and keeps original sentences as-is, while abstractive compression generates a fresh, shorter rewrite — trading some fidelity for conciseness.

cards.title: The Never-Ending Chat
cards.body: Some conversations with AI go on for hours or days — customer support, tutoring, companion apps. If the model tried to remember every single word forever, the context window would overflow fast. What's the fix?

cards.title: What Is a Rolling Summary?
cards.body: A rolling summary is a running recap of the conversation that gets updated periodically — say, every 10 turns. Old turns get folded into the summary and then dropped, so the context stays roughly the same size no matter how long the chat runs.

cards.title: The Diary Trick
cards.body: Imagine keeping a diary instead of remembering every single conversation word-for-word. Each night, you fold the day's events into one paragraph in your diary, then forget the tiny details. Next time you need context, you reread that paragraph instead of replaying the whole day.

cards.title: Customer Support Chat
cards.body: After 20 messages about a return request, the app compresses turns 1-15 into: 'User wants to return shoes bought March 2nd, size 9, unworn, has receipt.' The last 5 turns stay word-for-word. The next 10 turns later get folded into that same summary, updating it as needed.

cards.title: Myth: Summarize Once and Done
cards.body: It's not a one-time compression! Rolling summaries happen repeatedly, over and over, as the conversation keeps growing. Each time, the summary gets updated to absorb the newly-old turns, not replaced from scratch.

cards.title: Unbounded vs Rolling
cards.body: Without rolling summaries, context keeps growing turn after turn. With rolling summaries, old turns collapse into an updated recap, keeping size flat.

left.text: Context grows with every turn until it overflows or gets truncated

right.text: Old turns fold into a compact summary; size stays roughly constant over time

exercises.prompt: Why do rolling summaries help bound context growth in long conversations?

options.text: Because they delete the conversation after a fixed time limit
Because older turns are periodically compressed into a summary instead of being kept verbatim forever
Because they only work for conversations under 10 messages
Because they store every turn in a separate database and never touch the context window

exercises.explain: By folding old turns into an updated summary at regular intervals, the app avoids letting the transcript grow unboundedly — the context stays roughly flat in size no matter how long the chat continues.

exercises.prompt: True or False: With rolling summaries, the model still technically has access to the exact original wording of turns from an hour ago.
exercises.explain: False — once older turns are folded into the running summary, their exact wording is typically dropped. The model only sees the compressed summary, not the original verbatim text, which is the tradeoff for bounded context size.

exercises.sentence: Rolling summaries keep context size roughly constant by periodically folding older turns into a running summary.
exercises.explain: The whole point of a rolling summary is that it 'runs' continuously alongside the conversation, absorbing older content so nothing needs to be stored in full forever.

exercises.prompt: Which of these summaries was most likely AI-generated during a rolling summary update?

options.text: 'User confirmed shipping address as 42 Elm St, requested expedited delivery, and mentioned a prior order (#8821) had arrived late.'
'lol yeah that's so annoying, one time my package took 3 weeks too 😩'
'idk maybe just refund me? whatever works I guess'

exercises.explain: The first option reads like a neutral, structured recap pulling out key facts (address, request, order number) — exactly what an AI summarizer produces. The other two are casual, personal, opinionated turns typical of real human chat messages, not summaries.

exercises.prompt: A chatbot has been talking to a user for 3 hours across 200 turns. Using rolling summaries, what does its context window look like RIGHT NOW?

options.text: All 200 turns stored verbatim, growing forever
Only the very first message from 3 hours ago
A compact running summary of older turns, plus the most recent few turns in full
Nothing — the model has no memory of the conversation at all

exercises.explain: Rolling summaries work by periodically folding older turns into an updated summary, while keeping the newest turns verbatim for precision. This keeps context size roughly constant even as the conversation goes on forever, instead of growing without bound. This wraps up Context Compression — you now know how to extract, rewrite, and continuously roll up conversation history to keep long-running AI apps fast and affordable. Ready for the next unit? Let's keep building!
